citations: You can take only one Bonus Action on your turn, so you must choose which Bonus Action to use if you have more than one available.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly limit characters to a single Bonus Action per turn, regardless of how many different features grant them Bonus Action options.

citations: You also don’t provoke an Opportunity Attack when you Teleport or when you are moved without using your movement, action, Bonus Action, or Reaction.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly state that teleporting does not provoke an Opportunity Attack, so the wizard leaving the guard's reach in this manner does not trigger one.

citations: Breaking Up Your Move
answer.rationale: The combat rules explicitly state that you can break up your movement on your turn, using some of your Speed before and after your action.

citations: Moving between Attacks. If you move on your turn and have a feature, such as Extra Attack, that gives you more than one attack as part of the Attack action, you can use some or all of that movement to move between those attacks.
answer.rationale: The Attack action explicitly permits characters with the Extra Attack feature to use their movement to move between their attacks.

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 186
answer.rationale: The rules for Reactions explicitly state that you can take a Reaction on your own turn even if you have already taken an action, a Bonus Action, or both.

citations: Once you take a Reaction, you can’t take another one until the start of your next turn. The Opportunity Attack is a Reaction available to all creatures.
answer.rationale: A creature is limited to one Reaction and cannot take another one until the start of their next turn.

citations: Grappled [Condition]
answer.rationale: The Grappled condition does not prevent a creature from attacking; it only imposes Disadvantage on attack rolls made against targets other than the grappler.

citations:
One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn
On a turn, you can expend only one spell slot to cast a spell. This rule means you can’t, for example, cast a spell with a spell slot using the Magic action and another one using a Bonus Action on the same turn.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly limit a character to expending only one spell slot per turn to cast a spell, specifically prohibiting casting one spell with a slot as a Bonus Action and another with a slot using the Magic action on the same turn.

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 105
SRD 5.2.1 page 162
answer.rationale: The 'One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn' rule restricts a character to expending only one spell slot per turn. Since Shillelagh is a cantrip and does not expend a spell slot, it can be cast on the same turn as Cure Wounds, which expends the single permitted spell slot.

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 105
answer.rationale: The 'One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn' rule explicitly states that you can expend only one spell slot to cast a spell on a given turn. Because the wizard already expended a spell slot to cast Fireball, she cannot expend a second spell slot to cast Counterspell during that same turn.

citations: Another Concentration Effect. You lose Concentration on an effect the moment you start casting a spell that requires Concentration or activate another effect that requires Concentration.
answer.rationale: A character immediately loses Concentration on an active effect the moment they begin casting another spell that requires Concentration, even if they have not taken damage or failed a saving throw.

citations: When you Ready a spell, you cast it as normal (expending any resources used to cast it) but hold its energy, which you release with your Reaction when the trigger occurs.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly state that you expend the resources used to cast a spell at the time you Ready it, regardless of whether the trigger ever occurs.

citations: To be readied, a spell must have a casting time of an action, and holding on to the spell’s magic requires Concentration, which you can maintain up to the start of your next turn.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly state that a spell must have a casting time of an action in order to be readied, so a spell with a Bonus Action casting time like Misty Step cannot be readied.

citations: Automatic Critical Hits. Any attack roll that hits you is a Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of you.
answer.rationale: The Paralyzed condition only grants an automatic Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of the target, not when attacking from 30 feet away.

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 186
answer.rationale: Under the Prone condition, an attack roll against the target has Advantage only if the attacker is within 5 feet; otherwise, the attack roll has Disadvantage.

citations: A condition doesn’t stack with itself; a recipient either has a condition or doesn’t. The Exhaustion condition is an exception to that rule.
answer.rationale: The rules explicitly state that conditions do not stack with themselves, with Exhaustion being the only exception.

citations: Exhaustion [Condition]
answer.rationale: Under the Exhaustion condition, a D20 Test is reduced by 2 times the Exhaustion level (3 x 2 = 6) and Speed is reduced by 5 times the Exhaustion level (3 x 5 = 15 feet).

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 179
SRD 5.2.1 page 184
answer.rationale: The Incapacitated condition explicitly breaks a creature's Concentration immediately, regardless of whether any damage was taken or a saving throw was made.

citations: Invisible [Condition] ... Attacks Affected.  Attack rolls against you have Disadvantage, and your attack rolls have Advan - tage. If a creature can somehow see you, you don’t gain this benefit against that creature.
answer.rationale: The Invisible condition explicitly states that you do not gain advantage on attack rolls against a creature if that creature can somehow see you.

citations: To qualify for a new class, you must have a score of at least 13 in the primary ability of the new class and your current classes. For example, a Barbarian who decides to multiclass into the Druid class must have Strength and Wisdom scores of 13 or higher
answer.rationale: The character has a Wisdom score of 11, which falls below the minimum requirement of 13 needed to multiclass into the Druid class.

citations: If you gain the Extra Attack feature from more than one class, the features don’t stack. You can’t make more than two attacks with this feature unless you have a feature that says you can (such as the Fighter’s Two Extra Attacks feature).
answer.rationale: When gaining the Extra Attack feature from multiple classes, the features do not stack, limiting the character to two attacks per Attack action.

citations: SRD 5.2.1 page 177
answer.rationale: Under the Armor Training rules, if you wear Heavy armor and lack training with it, you cannot cast spells.

citations: To qualify for a new class, you must have a score of at least 13 in the primary ability of the new class and your current classes.
answer.rationale: The character meets the multiclassing prerequisites by having at least 13 in both the Fighter's primary ability (Strength) and the Wizard's primary ability (Intelligence).

citations: If circumstances cause a roll to have both Advantage and Disadvantage, the roll has neither of them, and you roll one d20. This is true even if multiple circumstances impose Disadvantage and only one grants Advantage or vice versa. In such a situation, you have neither Advantage nor Disadvantage.
answer.rationale: If a roll is affected by both Advantage and Disadvantage, the roll has neither of them. This rule applies regardless of whether the sources of Advantage outnumber the sources of Disadvantage.

citations: If multiple situations affect a roll and they all grant Advantage on it, you still roll only two d20s.
answer.rationale: Advantage does not stack, so no matter how many circumstances grant it, you roll only two d20s.

citations:
Interactions with Rerolls
When you have Advantage or Disadvantage and something in the game lets you reroll or replace the d20, you can reroll or replace only one die, not both. You choose which one.
answer.rationale: When a roll has Advantage and a feature allows a reroll, the player is strictly limited to rerolling only one of the two d20s, not both.
The Gloomherald patron subclass and its 'Duskmantle' feature are third-party content and are not found in the System Reference Document 5.2.1.

citations: Advantage and Disadvantage
Melee Attacks
answer.rationale: The SRD 5.2.1 does not contain a flanking mechanic; therefore, simply standing on opposite sides of an enemy does not inherently grant Advantage on attack rolls.

citations: Ability Checks
Influence
answer.rationale: A Charisma (Persuasion) check is not mind control, and players do not dictate the outcomes of ability checks. The DM determines whether a request is even possible, and a creature can be immune to the Influence action if a request is extreme or impossible.

citations: Combat: Damage and Healing: Critical Hits
answer.rationale: The SRD strictly defines a Critical Hit as rolling all of the attack's damage dice twice and adding them together, not tripling the damage.
